Outcomes.Text: Explain how conditional statements control the flow of execution in a Python program.
Apply for and while loops to iterate over sequences and repeat operations in engineering contexts.
Identify appropriate uses of break, continue, and the range() function within loop structures.
Demonstrate how to create and manipulate Python lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets.
Analyze the characteristics of each core data structure to distinguish when each is appropriate.
Evaluate engineering problems and select the most suitable Python data structure for a given scenario.

Topics.Title: Conditional Statements
Topics.Summary: Conditional statements allow a program to make decisions by executing different code blocks based on whether a condition evaluates to true or false. This topic covers if, elif, and else constructs in Python.

Elements.Title: The if Statement
Elements.BodyText: The if statement is the most fundamental conditional construct in Python, executing a block of code only when a specified condition is True.
Elements.Notes: Emphasize correct indentation as Python uses whitespace to define code blocks. A missing or incorrect indent is one of the most common beginner errors.

Items.Text: An if statement evaluates a Boolean expression and executes its indented block only when the result is True.
Python uses indentation (typically four spaces) rather than braces to define the scope of a conditional block.
Any expression that returns True or False can serve as the condition, including comparisons and logical operators.

Elements.Title: The elif Clause
Elements.BodyText: The elif (else-if) clause allows a program to check multiple conditions in sequence after an initial if condition fails.
Elements.Notes: Multiple elif clauses can be chained together. Python evaluates them top to bottom and executes only the first block whose condition is True, skipping all remaining elif and else blocks.

Items.Text: elif is evaluated only if all preceding if and elif conditions were False.
Any number of elif clauses can follow a single if statement.
Using elif instead of multiple independent if statements avoids unnecessary condition evaluations once a match is found.

Elements.Title: The else Clause
Elements.BodyText: The else clause defines a default block of code that executes when none of the preceding if or elif conditions are True.
Elements.Notes: An else clause is optional but provides a useful catch-all for handling unexpected or default cases, which is particularly valuable in engineering input validation.

Items.Text: else does not take a condition; it runs automatically when all prior conditions are False.
Only one else clause is permitted per if-elif-else chain, and it must appear last.
Including an else clause is good practice for handling edge cases and unexpected inputs.

Elements.Title: Comparison and Logical Operators
Elements.BodyText: Conditions in Python are built using comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) and logical operators (and, or, not) to form complex Boolean expressions.
Elements.Notes: Remind learners that == tests equality while = is assignment. Mixing these up is a very common source of bugs. The 'not' operator inverts a Boolean value.

Items.Text: Comparison operators return True or False by comparing two values.
The and operator returns True only if both operands are True.
The or operator returns True if at least one operand is True.
The not operator negates a Boolean expression, turning True to False and vice versa.

Elements.Title: Nested Conditionals
Elements.BodyText: Conditional statements can be placed inside other conditional blocks to test additional conditions when an outer condition is True.
Elements.Notes: While nesting is sometimes necessary, deeply nested conditionals reduce readability. Consider refactoring with elif or combining conditions using logical operators where possible.

Items.Text: A nested conditional is an if statement contained within the block of another if, elif, or else clause.
Each nested level requires an additional level of indentation.
Excessive nesting can make code harder to read and maintain; flattening logic with logical operators is often preferred.

Topics.Title: for Loops and the range() Function
Topics.Summary: The for loop iterates over a sequence of items, making it ideal for processing collections of data. The range() function generates numeric sequences commonly used to control loop iterations.

Elements.Title: The for Loop
Elements.BodyText: A for loop in Python iterates over each item in a sequence — such as a list, string, or range — executing the loop body once per item.
Elements.Notes: Unlike C or Java, Python's for loop is inherently a 'for-each' loop. It does not require managing an index variable manually unless range() is used.

Items.Text: The loop variable takes on the value of each successive item in the iterable on each iteration.
Any iterable object — including lists, tuples, strings, and dictionaries — can be used with a for loop.
The loop body is indented and executes once for every item in the sequence.

Elements.Title: The range() Function
Elements.BodyText: The range() function generates an immutable sequence of integers and is commonly used with for loops to repeat an action a specific number of times.
Elements.Notes: range(start, stop, step) — the stop value is exclusive. For example, range(0, 10, 2) produces 0, 2, 4, 6, 8. A negative step can be used to count down.

Items.Text: range(n) generates integers from 0 up to but not including n.
range(start, stop) generates integers from start up to but not including stop.
range(start, stop, step) generates integers from start to stop, incrementing by step each time.
range() produces values lazily, making it memory-efficient for large sequences.

Elements.Title: Iterating with an Index Using enumerate()
Elements.BodyText: The enumerate() function allows a for loop to access both the index and the value of each item in an iterable simultaneously.
Elements.Notes: enumerate() is preferred over manually maintaining a counter variable. It returns (index, value) tuples and accepts an optional start parameter to offset the index.

Items.Text: enumerate(iterable) returns pairs of (index, value) for each item in the sequence.
Using enumerate() avoids the need for a separate counter variable.
The start parameter of enumerate() allows the index to begin at a value other than zero.

Elements.Title: Looping Over Numeric Ranges in Engineering
Elements.BodyText: Engineering applications frequently require iterating over a defined numeric range, such as time steps in a simulation or indices in a data array.
Elements.Notes: Example: iterating over sensor readings at fixed time intervals, or summing force values across discrete structural nodes. The range() function maps directly to such use cases.

Items.Text: range() is ideal for stepping through evenly spaced numeric intervals common in engineering computations.
Combining range() with len() allows iteration over indices of any list or array.
Careful selection of start, stop, and step values prevents off-by-one errors in numerical simulations.

Topics.Title: while Loops
Topics.Summary: A while loop repeats a block of code for as long as a specified condition remains True, making it suitable for situations where the number of iterations is not known in advance.

Elements.Title: The while Loop Structure
Elements.BodyText: A while loop continuously executes its body until its controlling condition evaluates to False, checking the condition before each iteration.
Elements.Notes: Always ensure the loop body contains logic that will eventually make the condition False, otherwise the loop will run indefinitely, causing a program hang.

Items.Text: The condition is evaluated before each iteration; if it is False initially, the loop body never executes.
The loop body must include a statement that changes the condition's outcome to eventually terminate the loop.
while loops are well suited for event-driven loops, convergence checks, and user-input validation.

Elements.Title: Infinite Loops and Loop Guards
Elements.BodyText: An infinite loop occurs when a while loop's condition never becomes False, causing the program to run indefinitely without halting.
Elements.Notes: Infinite loops can be intentional (e.g., a server listening for connections) and controlled via break. In most computational engineering contexts they are accidental and must be avoided.

Items.Text: An infinite loop results when the loop condition is never updated to become False.
Common causes include forgetting to increment a counter or using an always-True condition.
A loop guard is a variable or condition that ensures the loop will eventually terminate.

Elements.Title: Comparing for and while Loops
Elements.BodyText: Choosing between a for loop and a while loop depends on whether the number of iterations is known in advance or determined dynamically during execution.
Elements.Notes: Rule of thumb: use for when iterating over a known collection or fixed count; use while when continuing until a condition changes, such as convergence in an iterative solver.

Items.Text: Use a for loop when the number of iterations or the sequence to iterate over is known before the loop starts.
Use a while loop when the loop must continue until a runtime condition changes.
Both loop types can solve many of the same problems, but choosing appropriately improves code clarity.

Topics.Title: Loop Control: break and continue
Topics.Summary: The break and continue statements provide fine-grained control over loop execution, allowing early termination or skipping specific iterations without exiting the loop entirely.

Elements.Title: The break Statement
Elements.BodyText: The break statement immediately terminates the innermost loop it appears in, transferring control to the first statement after the loop.
Elements.Notes: break is commonly used to exit a search loop once a target value is found, or to stop a while loop when a sentinel condition is met. In nested loops, break only exits the innermost loop.

Items.Text: break exits the loop immediately, regardless of whether the loop condition is still True.
Execution continues with the first statement after the loop's closing block.
In nested loops, break only terminates the innermost loop in which it appears.
break is often paired with an if statement to conditionally exit a loop.

Elements.Title: The continue Statement
Elements.BodyText: The continue statement skips the remainder of the current loop iteration and jumps directly to the next iteration's condition check.
Elements.Notes: continue is useful for filtering out unwanted values during iteration, such as skipping invalid sensor readings or zero-valued entries in a dataset.

Items.Text: continue does not exit the loop; it only skips the rest of the current iteration.
After continue, a for loop moves to the next item and a while loop re-evaluates its condition.
continue improves readability by avoiding deeply nested if statements inside loop bodies.

Elements.Title: The else Clause on Loops
Elements.BodyText: Python allows an optional else clause on both for and while loops, which executes only if the loop completed normally without encountering a break statement.
Elements.Notes: This feature is unique to Python and is useful for search algorithms — the else block signals that no break was triggered, meaning the search item was not found.

Items.Text: A loop's else block runs after the loop finishes all iterations without hitting a break.
If break is executed, the else block is skipped entirely.
The loop else clause is particularly useful for implementing search logic with a clear 'not found' outcome.

Elements.Title: Practical Use Cases for break and continue
Elements.BodyText: In engineering and data processing, break and continue are frequently used to handle exceptions in data, implement early-exit optimizations, and process conditional sequences.
Elements.Notes: Example: iterating through a list of measurement values and using continue to skip negative (invalid) readings, or using break to stop processing once a threshold exceedance is detected.

Items.Text: Use break to stop iterating once a target condition or result is found, saving unnecessary computation.
Use continue to skip invalid, missing, or out-of-range values during data processing.
Combining break and continue with conditional logic creates efficient, readable loop structures.

Topics.Title: Lists
Topics.Summary: Lists are ordered, mutable sequences that can hold items of any type, making them one of the most versatile and widely used data structures in Python.

Elements.Title: Creating and Accessing Lists
Elements.BodyText: A list is created using square brackets and can store any mix of data types. Individual elements are accessed using zero-based integer indices.
Elements.Notes: Negative indices count from the end of the list: my_list[-1] returns the last element. This is a common Python idiom worth highlighting.

Items.Text: Lists are defined with square brackets: my_list = [1, 2, 3].
Elements are accessed by index starting at 0: my_list[0] returns the first element.
Negative indices access elements from the end of the list.
Lists can contain elements of different data types, including other lists.

Elements.Title: Modifying Lists
Elements.BodyText: Because lists are mutable, their elements can be changed, added, or removed after the list is created using built-in methods and direct assignment.
Elements.Notes: Key methods: append() adds one item to the end, extend() adds multiple items, insert() adds at a specific index, remove() deletes by value, pop() removes by index and returns the value.

Items.Text: Direct index assignment (my_list[0] = 10) replaces an existing element.
append() adds a single element to the end of the list.
remove() deletes the first occurrence of a specified value.
pop() removes and returns the element at a given index, defaulting to the last element.

Elements.Title: List Slicing
Elements.BodyText: Slicing extracts a sub-list from a list using the syntax list[start:stop:step], returning a new list containing the selected elements.
Elements.Notes: Slicing does not modify the original list. Omitting start defaults to 0; omitting stop defaults to the end of the list. A step of -1 reverses the list.

Items.Text: The syntax list[start:stop] returns elements from index start up to but not including stop.
An optional step value controls how many indices to skip between selected elements.
Slicing creates a shallow copy of the selected portion, leaving the original list unchanged.

Elements.Title: List Comprehensions
Elements.BodyText: A list comprehension provides a concise, readable way to create a new list by applying an expression to each item in an iterable, optionally filtered by a condition.
Elements.Notes: Syntax: [expression for item in iterable if condition]. Example: squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]. List comprehensions are generally faster and more Pythonic than equivalent for loops.

Items.Text: List comprehensions replace many for-loop-based list-building patterns with a single line of code.
An optional if clause filters which items from the iterable are included.
List comprehensions improve readability and are considered idiomatic Python.

Elements.Title: Common List Operations and Methods
Elements.BodyText: Python provides a rich set of built-in functions and list methods for sorting, searching, counting, and transforming list data.
Elements.Notes: Useful functions: len(), sum(), min(), max(), sorted(). Useful methods: list.sort(), list.reverse(), list.count(), list.index(). Note that sort() modifies in place while sorted() returns a new list.

Items.Text: len() returns the number of elements in a list.
sort() rearranges list elements in place; sorted() returns a new sorted list without modifying the original.
count() returns how many times a specific value appears in the list.
index() returns the position of the first occurrence of a specified value.

Topics.Title: Tuples
Topics.Summary: Tuples are ordered, immutable sequences that are ideal for storing fixed collections of data that should not be changed after creation.

Elements.Title: Creating and Accessing Tuples
Elements.BodyText: Tuples are defined using parentheses (or simply commas) and support the same indexing and slicing operations as lists, but their contents cannot be modified.
Elements.Notes: A single-element tuple requires a trailing comma: my_tuple = (42,). Without the comma, Python treats the parentheses as a grouping operator, not a tuple.

Items.Text: Tuples are created with parentheses: my_tuple = (1, 2, 3), or without: my_tuple = 1, 2, 3.
Elements are accessed by zero-based index, identical to list indexing.
A single-element tuple must include a trailing comma to distinguish it from a parenthesized expression.

Elements.Title: Immutability of Tuples
Elements.BodyText: Once a tuple is created, its elements cannot be added, removed, or changed, making tuples a safer choice when data integrity is important.
Elements.Notes: Immutability means tuples are hashable (if all elements are hashable) and can be used as dictionary keys or set members, unlike lists.

Items.Text: Attempting to assign a new value to a tuple element raises a TypeError.
Immutability guarantees the data remains consistent throughout program execution.
Because tuples are immutable, they are hashable and can be used as dictionary keys.

Elements.Title: Tuple Packing and Unpacking
Elements.BodyText: Tuple packing assigns multiple values into a single tuple, while tuple unpacking assigns the elements of a tuple to individual variables in one statement.
Elements.Notes: Unpacking is widely used in Python for swapping variables (a, b = b, a) and for receiving multiple return values from a function.

Items.Text: Packing: coordinates = (10, 20) stores two values in one tuple.
Unpacking: x, y = coordinates assigns each element to a separate variable.
The number of variables on the left must match the number of elements in the tuple unless a starred expression is used.
Functions that return multiple values implicitly return a tuple.

Elements.Title: When to Use Tuples vs. Lists
Elements.BodyText: Tuples are preferred when the data represents a fixed record or should be protected from modification, while lists are better suited for collections that need to grow or change.
Elements.Notes: Engineering examples: store (x, y, z) coordinates as a tuple since spatial coordinates are conceptually fixed; store a series of timestep values as a list since they may be filtered or sorted.

Items.Text: Use a tuple when the number and meaning of elements are fixed, such as a coordinate pair or RGB color value.
Use a list when items will be added, removed, or reordered during execution.
Tuples communicate intent to other developers that the data is not meant to change.

Topics.Title: Dictionaries
Topics.Summary: Dictionaries store data as key-value pairs, enabling fast lookups by key rather than by position, and are ideal for representing structured, labeled data.

Elements.Title: Creating and Accessing Dictionaries
Elements.BodyText: Dictionaries are created using curly braces with colon-separated key-value pairs. Values are retrieved by supplying the corresponding key in square brackets.
Elements.Notes: Keys must be immutable (e.g., strings, numbers, tuples) and unique within a dictionary. Using a key that does not exist raises a KeyError; the get() method avoids this by returning None or a default value.

Items.Text: Dictionaries are defined as: my_dict = {'key': value, ...}.
Values are accessed with my_dict['key']; using a missing key raises a KeyError.
The get() method returns a default value instead of raising an error when a key is not found.
Dictionary keys must be unique; assigning to an existing key overwrites its value.

Elements.Title: Modifying Dictionaries
Elements.BodyText: Dictionaries are mutable; entries can be added, updated, or deleted at any time using direct assignment and built-in methods.
Elements.Notes: del my_dict['key'] removes a key-value pair. pop('key') removes and returns the value. update() merges another dictionary into the current one.

Items.Text: Assigning to a new key (my_dict['new_key'] = value) adds the entry to the dictionary.
Assigning to an existing key replaces its current value.
del removes a key-value pair; pop() removes and returns the value for a specified key.
update() merges key-value pairs from another dictionary, overwriting duplicate keys.

Elements.Title: Iterating Over Dictionaries
Elements.BodyText: Python provides dedicated methods for iterating over a dictionary's keys, values, or key-value pairs using keys(), values(), and items().
Elements.Notes: As of Python 3.7+, dictionaries maintain insertion order. Iterating with items() returns (key, value) tuples, which can be unpacked directly in a for loop.

Items.Text: keys() returns a view of all keys in the dictionary.
values() returns a view of all values in the dictionary.
items() returns a view of all (key, value) pairs, suitable for unpacking in a for loop.

Elements.Title: Engineering Applications of Dictionaries
Elements.BodyText: Dictionaries are well suited for mapping labeled engineering data, such as material properties, configuration parameters, or named measurement channels.
Elements.Notes: Example: {'Young_modulus': 200e9, 'yield_strength': 250e6, 'density': 7850} stores steel properties with descriptive string keys, making code self-documenting.

Items.Text: Dictionaries allow data to be accessed by a meaningful label rather than a numeric index.
They are ideal for storing configuration settings, lookup tables, and named datasets.
Using descriptive string keys makes engineering code easier to read and maintain.

Topics.Title: Sets and Choosing the Right Data Structure
Topics.Summary: Sets store unique, unordered elements and support efficient membership testing and set operations. This topic also provides guidance on selecting the appropriate data structure for engineering problems.

Elements.Title: Creating and Using Sets
Elements.BodyText: A set is an unordered collection of unique elements defined with curly braces or the set() constructor. It automatically discards duplicate values.
Elements.Notes: To create an empty set, use set() — not {} which creates an empty dictionary. Sets are mutable but their elements must be immutable (hashable).

Items.Text: Sets are created with curly braces: my_set = {1, 2, 3}, or with set().
Duplicate values are automatically removed; each element appears only once.
An empty set must be created with set(), not {}, which creates an empty dictionary.

Elements.Title: Set Operations
Elements.BodyText: Sets support mathematical operations including union, intersection, difference, and symmetric difference, enabling powerful comparisons between groups of data.
Elements.Notes: Operations can be applied using methods (union(), intersection()) or operators (|, &, -, ^). These are useful in engineering for comparing sensor lists, active flags, or categories.

Items.Text: Union (| or union()) returns all elements present in either set.
Intersection (& or intersection()) returns only elements present in both sets.
Difference (- or difference()) returns elements in the first set not found in the second.
Symmetric difference (^ or symmetric_difference()) returns elements in one set but not both.

Elements.Title: Membership Testing with Sets
Elements.BodyText: Sets provide O(1) average-time membership testing using the in operator, making them significantly faster than lists for checking whether a value exists in a large collection.
Elements.Notes: For large datasets where membership testing is frequent, converting a list to a set before repeated lookups can provide a substantial performance improvement.

Items.Text: The in operator tests whether an element exists in a set in constant average time.
Membership testing in a set is much faster than in a list for large collections.
Sets are the preferred structure when the primary operation is checking for the presence of unique items.

Elements.Title: Comparing Core Data Structures
Elements.BodyText: Lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets each have distinct properties — ordered vs. unordered, mutable vs. immutable, indexed vs. keyed — that make them appropriate for different tasks.
Elements.Notes: Quick reference: List — ordered, mutable, indexed by position; Tuple — ordered, immutable, indexed by position; Dictionary — unordered (insertion-ordered 3.7+), mutable, keyed; Set — unordered, mutable, unique elements only.

Items.Text: Lists are best for ordered, changeable sequences accessed by position.
Tuples are best for fixed records where immutability and hashability are required.
Dictionaries are best for labeled data requiring fast lookup by a meaningful key.
Sets are best for storing unique items and performing membership tests or set algebra.

Elements.Title: Selecting the Right Structure for Engineering Problems
Elements.BodyText: Choosing the appropriate data structure improves program correctness, performance, and readability, particularly in data-intensive engineering applications.
Elements.Notes: Guide learners to ask: Do I need order? Do items need labels? Must values be unique? Will the collection change? Answers to these questions lead directly to the correct structure.

Items.Text: Ask whether the data needs to be ordered and accessed by position — if yes, consider a list or tuple.
Ask whether the data needs to be accessed by a descriptive label — if yes, use a dictionary.
Ask whether uniqueness of elements is required or membership testing will be frequent — if yes, use a set.
Ask whether the data should be protected from modification — if yes, prefer a tuple over a list.
